SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main technical claims (that multi-criteria shared-private architectures and adversarial training improve CWS) are supported by multi-corpus experiments (see description of Table 4) and the formulations (CRF inference in Eq.7-10, adversarial losses in Eq.20-21 and combined objective Eq.22) are standard and appear correct. Mathematical derivations are straightforward and consistent with existing work (Bi-LSTM+CRF). Minor issues include lack of statistical significance testing and some heuristic training choices (e.g. training 2400 epochs on eight batches then fixing Θs) that are not fully justified, which slightly weakens confidence but do not invalidate the results.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines known ideas—shared/private multi-task architectures and adversarial domain-invariance—with Bi-LSTM+CRF for Chinese word segmentation. The authors situate the work relative to prior neural multi-view and multi-task efforts (they cite Chen et al., 2016 and Ganin et al., 2016) and propose three specific sharing variants, which is a moderate extension but not a fundamentally new technique. An expert would view this as an incremental, application-focused novelty rather than a major methodological breakthrough.

CLARITY.justification: The high-level organization (embedding -> feature layers -> CRF -> multi-criteria models -> adversarial training -> experiments) is clear and equations are well-placed (e.g. Eqs. 1,7-10,12-14,18,20-22). However the provided text contains repeated paragraphs, formatting issues and some ambiguous exposition (e.g. exact hyperparameter values and some duplicated sentences), which makes parts of the paper harder to follow and harms polish.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are extensive in terms of datasets (eight SIGHAN corpora) and evaluate multiple model variants (Model-I/II/III, with and without adversarial training), and also test traditional vs simplified Chinese transfer. However, important experimental details and reproducibility information are incomplete (precise hyperparameters, model sizes, random seeds), there are no statistical significance tests, and failure cases/limitations are only briefly discussed, limiting full depth.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a relevant and practical problem (multi-criteria CWS) and shows consistent improvements across multiple standard corpora, so it is likely to be useful to researchers/practitioners working on Chinese segmentation and multi-criteria learning. Nonetheless the contribution is incremental (combining existing techniques) so it is unlikely to be transformative beyond this subfield.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (neural methods for Chinese word segmentation and multi-criteria/multi-task learning) is squarely within the scope of top NLP conferences and uses standard methods and evaluations expected by that community (SIGHAN datasets, Bi-LSTM+CRF baselines). The framing and experiments are appropriate for an NLP/ML venue, with minor concerns about experimental reporting detail.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against reasonable neural baselines (Bi-LSTM and stacked Bi-LSTM) and situates itself relative to prior multi-criteria and multi-view works (e.g. Chen et al., 2016). However it is unclear whether it compares to the strongest state-of-the-art systems for each dataset or reports statistical significance; some competing approaches mentioned in related work are not quantitatively included in the main tables, which limits the completeness of comparisons.
